def: A distressing inner restlessness with a compulsion to move (pacing, rocking) — a common, often early antipsychotic side effect.
A sudden, sustained, often painful muscle spasm (neck, jaw, eyes, throat) — an early high-potency antipsychotic reaction; laryngospasm can threaten the airway.
Antipsychotic-induced tremor, rigidity, and slowed movement mimicking Parkinson disease, from D2 blockade in the striatum.
Drug-induced movement disturbances from dopamine (D2) blockade — dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, and (late) tardive dyskinesia.
Involuntary repetitive movements (often tongue, lips, face) emerging after prolonged D2 blockade — may be irreversible; anticholinergics worsen it.
A potentially fatal excess of serotonin: clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation, sweating, and fever — usually from combining serotonergic drugs.
A rare, life-threatening reaction to dopamine blockers: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mental status, autonomic instability, and raised CK.
Withdrawal symptoms (dizziness, flu-like feelings, 'brain zaps') after abruptly stopping a short-half-life antidepressant; taper to prevent.
The cluster of weight gain, high glucose, and dyslipidemia driven by some antipsychotics (worst with olanzapine/clozapine); monitor weight, glucose, lipids.
Toxidrome from blocking acetylcholine: dry flushed hot skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, and delirium — 'mad, red, hot, dry, blind'.
Poisoning from a high lithium level: coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, and GI upset; severe cases need IV saline and hemodialysis.
A hypertensive emergency when MAOIs meet tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meats) or a sympathomimetic — the classic 'cheese reaction'.
Lengthening of the heart's QT interval on ECG, raising the risk of torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia triggered by a prolonged QT interval; can degenerate into fatal arrhythmia.
Dangerously slow, shallow breathing — the lethal mechanism in opioid, benzodiazepine, and barbiturate overdose, worsened by combining them.
Inability to form new memories over a period — a known effect of benzodiazepines and Z-drugs, especially short-acting ones.
A drop in blood pressure on standing causing light-headedness or falls — often from alpha-1 blockade; counsel patients to rise slowly.
Drug-induced drowsiness and reduced alertness (CNS depression); additive and dangerous when sedatives are combined.
Rhythmic involuntary shaking; a fine tremor is common with lithium/SSRIs, while a coarse tremor can signal lithium toxicity.
Low blood sodium, often from SIADH with SSRIs or carbamazepine; causes confusion, falls, and seizures — check early in the elderly.
Syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion: excess water retention causing dilutional hyponatremia — a known SSRI/carbamazepine effect.
A severe drop in neutrophils leaving the patient defenceless against infection — the key clozapine and carbamazepine danger; monitor the ANC.
Inflammation of heart muscle; a rare but serious clozapine risk peaking in the first 6-8 weeks — watch for fatigue, fever, chest pain, raised troponin.
A prolonged, painful erection unrelated to arousal — a urologic emergency; classically caused by trazodone via alpha-1 blockade.
Inappropriate breast-milk production — a sign of hyperprolactinemia from dopamine-blocking antipsychotics.
Absence of menstrual periods; with antipsychotics it signals prolactin elevation from D2 blockade.
Elevated prolactin from dopamine (D2) blockade, causing galactorrhea, amenorrhea, sexual dysfunction, and reduced bone density.
Ratchet-like resistance felt when passively moving a limb — a parkinsonian sign, here from antipsychotic D2 blockade.
Muscle stiffness; generalized 'lead-pipe' rigidity is a hallmark of neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes; with clonus, a key sign pointing to serotonin syndrome rather than NMS.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions (often ankle or eyes); spontaneous or ocular clonus is a hallmark of serotonin syndrome.
Profuse sweating; an autonomic sign in serotonin syndrome, NMS, and withdrawal states.
Dangerously high body temperature; a life-threatening feature of serotonin syndrome and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Loss of coordinated movement and unsteady gait; here a sign of lithium toxicity or benzodiazepine excess.
Dangerous swings in heart rate, blood pressure, and temperature; a feature of NMS, serotonin syndrome, and delirium tremens.
Visible slowing of movement, speech, and thought — a feature of severe depression.
Excessive sleepiness or sleeping too much; can be a feature of atypical depression.
A pervasive lack of energy; a common feature of depression.
Pupil dilation; here a sign of opioid withdrawal (pinpoint pupils instead point to opioid intoxication).
Markedly constricted pupils — a classic sign of opioid intoxication or overdose.
Passing large volumes of urine; an early sign of lithium's renal effect (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus).
Excessive thirst and drinking, often paired with polyuria; seen with lithium's effect on the kidney.
A mottled, net-like purplish skin discoloration; a characteristic amantadine side effect.
Tingling or 'pins-and-needles' sensation; a characteristic topiramate side effect.
Feeling detached from reality (derealization) or from oneself (depersonalization); can occur during panic attacks.
'Goosebumps' from hair-muscle contraction; a classic autonomic sign of opioid withdrawal.
Reduced libido, arousal, or delayed orgasm — a common SSRI/SNRI effect; less with bupropion and mirtazapine.
Difficulty emptying the bladder from anticholinergic or noradrenergic drug effects; worse in older men with prostatic enlargement.
Heightened skin sensitivity to sunlight causing easy sunburn; classic with chlorpromazine — counsel sun protection.
Sleep-walking, sleep-driving, or sleep-eating with no memory of it — linked to Z-drugs (a boxed warning for zolpidem).
Seizures on abruptly stopping a sedative (benzodiazepine, barbiturate, alcohol) — a key reason to taper rather than stop suddenly.
A distorted sense of taste; the characteristic metallic aftertaste of zopiclone and a local effect of sublingual asenapine.
Numbness of the mouth and tongue — a characteristic local effect of the sublingual antipsychotic asenapine.
Withdrawal symptoms in a newborn exposed in utero to a drug such as opioids, antidepressants, or benzodiazepines.
Drug-induced liver injury; a concern with valproate, duloxetine, and naltrexone — monitor liver enzymes.
Inflammation of the pancreas (severe abdominal pain, raised lipase); a recognized valproate adverse effect warranting drug discontinuation.
A sudden painful rise in eye pressure threatening vision; an idiosyncratic topiramate reaction, usually in the first month.
Kidney resistance to ADH causing large volumes of dilute urine and thirst — a chronic lithium effect.
A congenital malformation of the tricuspid valve linked to first-trimester lithium exposure (absolute risk is low).
Serious congenital malformations of the brain or spine (e.g. spina bifida) linked to valproate exposure in pregnancy.
A rare, life-threatening blistering skin/mucosal reaction; lamotrigine's slow titration and carbamazepine HLA screening exist to prevent it.
Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms — a severe delayed hypersensitivity (e.g. carbamazepine; HLA-A*3101 predicts it).
Excess acid in the blood; topiramate causes a non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis via carbonic-anhydrase inhibition.
Anemia with abnormally large red cells from impaired DNA synthesis; a chronic phenobarbital effect via folate interference.
A neurodevelopmental disorder of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity that impairs daily functioning.
A mood disorder of persistent low mood or loss of interest plus neurovegetative symptoms lasting at least two weeks and impairing function.
A distinct period of abnormally elevated or irritable mood with high energy, reduced sleep need, and risky behaviour — defining bipolar I.
A milder, shorter form of mania without psychosis or major functional impairment — defining bipolar II.
Bipolar disorder with four or more mood episodes in a year; antidepressants can worsen it.
Depressive and manic symptoms occurring together in one episode, raising suicide risk and complicating treatment.
A chronic psychotic disorder with hallucinations, delusions, disorganization, and negative symptoms.
A loss of contact with reality — hallucinations, delusions, or disorganized thinking — seen across many conditions.
Schizophrenia failing two or more adequate antipsychotic trials; clozapine is the only agent with proven superior efficacy.
Hallucinations and delusions in Parkinson disease, often drug-related; treated without worsening motor symptoms (pimavanserin, quetiapine).
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness from a medical cause — usually reversible; benzodiazepines often worsen it.
A progressive decline in memory and other cognitive domains that impairs independence (e.g. Alzheimer disease).

aliases: behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia
def: Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia — agitation, aggression, psychosis, wandering; try non-drug measures first.
A behavioural emergency of heightened arousal, restlessness, and potential aggression needing rapid, safe calming.
A chronic, relapsing pattern of compulsive alcohol use with tolerance, withdrawal, and impaired control despite harm.
Tremor, anxiety, and autonomic arousal after stopping heavy drinking; severe cases progress to seizures or delirium tremens.
The most severe alcohol withdrawal: confusion, agitation, fever, and autonomic instability 2-4 days after the last drink — a medical emergency.
A chronic pattern of compulsive opioid use with tolerance, withdrawal, and loss of control; treated with opioid agonist therapy.
A distressing but non-lethal syndrome — yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, myalgia, diarrhea, and dilated pupils — on stopping opioids.
A life-threatening opioid effect: depressed breathing, pinpoint pupils, and unresponsiveness — reversed with naloxone.
Maintenance of opioid use disorder with a long-acting opioid (methadone or buprenorphine) to prevent withdrawal and craving.
Abrupt, severe withdrawal triggered when a partial agonist (buprenorphine) displaces a full opioid from receptors; time the first dose carefully.
Nicotine dependence with compulsive smoking, difficulty quitting, and a withdrawal syndrome; highly treatable with medication plus support.
Persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep despite adequate opportunity, with resulting daytime impairment.
Chronic, excessive, hard-to-control worry across many areas, with restlessness, tension, and sleep disturbance.
Recurrent unexpected surges of intense fear with physical symptoms (palpitations, breathlessness, derealization) peaking within minutes.
Marked fear of social or performance situations where one might be scrutinized or judged.
Intrusive, unwanted obsessions with repetitive compulsions done to reduce distress; needs higher SSRI doses and longer trials than depression.
A trauma-related disorder with intrusive memories or nightmares, avoidance, hyperarousal, and negative mood after a traumatic event.
Depression arising in pregnancy or after delivery; part of the spectrum of perinatal mood and anxiety disorders.
Depression that fails to respond to two or more adequate antidepressant trials; options include esketamine and ECT.
A syndrome of psychomotor disturbance (immobility, mutism, posturing, or excitement) treated with benzodiazepines or ECT.
A prolonged or repeated seizure without recovery of consciousness — a neurologic emergency; IV lorazepam is first-line.
A chronic disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness with abnormal REM intrusion, caused by deficiency of wake-promoting orexin.
A neurodevelopmental disorder of chronic motor and vocal tics beginning in childhood.
A circadian rhythm disorder in which sleep onset and waking are shifted markedly later than desired.
Acting out dreams from loss of the normal muscle paralysis of REM sleep; can herald neurodegenerative disease.
Involuntary bedwetting during sleep; historically treated in children with imipramine.
Pain from nerve damage or dysfunction (e.g. diabetic neuropathy), often responsive to SNRIs, TCAs, or gabapentinoids.
A chronic widespread-pain syndrome with fatigue and sleep disturbance; duloxetine and milnacipran are used.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half — it drives dosing frequency, accumulation, and withdrawal risk.
Gradually adjusting a drug's dose up or down to balance efficacy against tolerability.
An inactive compound the body converts into the active drug (e.g. lisdexamfetamine to dextroamphetamine).
A pharmacologically active breakdown product of a drug that can prolong or add to its effects.
Extensive breakdown of an oral drug by the gut and liver before it reaches the bloodstream, lowering its bioavailability.
The fraction of a dose that reaches the bloodstream unchanged; reduced by first-pass metabolism or, for lurasidone, taking it without food.
The point where drug intake equals elimination and blood levels plateau — usually reached after about 4-5 half-lives.
A liver enzyme metabolizing many antidepressants and antipsychotics; inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine) and genetics alter drug levels.
A major liver/gut enzyme; inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, grapefruit) raise, and inducers (carbamazepine) lower, levels of its substrates.
A liver enzyme inhibited by fluvoxamine and induced by cigarette smoke; it clears clozapine, olanzapine, and caffeine.
A liver enzyme metabolizing citalopram/escitalopram and clobazam; genetic 'poor metabolizers' need lower doses.
A drug speeding up a metabolic enzyme, lowering levels of drugs it clears and risking lost efficacy (e.g. carbamazepine, smoking).
A drug blocking a metabolic enzyme, raising levels of drugs it clears and risking toxicity (e.g. fluvoxamine on CYP1A2).
The gap between a drug's effective dose and its toxic dose; narrow-window drugs (lithium, TCAs) need blood-level monitoring.
Measuring blood drug levels to guide dosing for narrow-window drugs like lithium, clozapine, and some TCAs.
A drug-free interval letting one medication clear before starting another — critical between MAOIs and serotonergic drugs.
A drug that activates a receptor only partially, giving a submaximal effect with a ceiling (e.g. aripiprazole at D2, buprenorphine at mu).
A drug that binds a receptor and produces the opposite effect of an agonist, lowering baseline activity (e.g. pimavanserin at 5-HT2A).
A dose above which more drug adds no further effect; buprenorphine's ceiling on respiratory depression makes overdose harder.
How strongly a drug activates a receptor once bound; lower intrinsic activity at D2 (brexpiprazole) means less activation than aripiprazole.
One of two mirror-image forms of a molecule; the single active form may be marketed alone (escitalopram, esketamine, armodafinil).
A large initial dose given to reach a therapeutic level quickly; valproate can be loaded for acute mania.
The most serious drug-label safety warning, highlighting a potentially life-threatening risk.
A drug with restricted prescribing due to abuse and dependence potential (e.g. stimulants, benzodiazepines).
A slow-release injectable antipsychotic given every few weeks to support adherence when daily pills are unreliable.
An oily ester formulation of an antipsychotic that dissolves slowly from muscle to give a long-acting depot injection.
A liver conjugation pathway (not CYP) that clears lorazepam, oxazepam, and temazepam — preferred in liver disease.
A drug that blocks the transporter recycling a neurotransmitter, raising its synaptic levels (e.g. SSRIs block serotonin reuptake).
The main opioid receptor; agonists at it relieve pain but cause euphoria and respiratory depression.
The brain's susceptibility to seizures; some drugs (bupropion, clozapine, high-dose TCAs) lower it, raising seizure risk.
A drug blocking acetylcholine's muscarinic effects — causing dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, and confusion.
The blood count of neutrophils; serial ANC monitoring on clozapine catches dangerous drops (agranulocytosis).
A genetic marker (common in some Asian ancestries) strongly predicting carbamazepine-induced Stevens-Johnson syndrome; screen before starting.
A score grading the severity of liver cirrhosis (class A-C) that guides drug choice and dosing.
Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale — scores withdrawal severity to time buprenorphine induction and avoid precipitated withdrawal.
Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy — a regulated safety program controlling access to higher-risk drugs (e.g. esketamine, clozapine).
A diagnostic rule for serotonin syndrome: a serotonergic drug plus clonus or specific autonomic/neuromuscular signs.
Inducing a brief therapeutic seizure under anesthesia; a leading option for severe, psychotic, or emergency depression.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor — first-line antidepressants that block serotonin reuptake (e.g. sertraline, escitalopram).
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — antidepressants raising both serotonin and noradrenaline (e.g. venlafaxine, duloxetine).
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor — an older antidepressant class requiring a tyramine-restricted diet and drug washouts (e.g. phenelzine).
Reversible inhibitor of MAO-A (moclobemide) — an MAOI subtype needing no strict tyramine diet, since tyramine can displace it.
An older antidepressant class; effective but lethal in overdose (cardiac sodium-channel blockade) with heavy anticholinergic effects.
A GABA-A enhancer used for anxiety, insomnia, seizures, and withdrawal; carries tolerance, dependence, and sedation risk.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) acting at the benzodiazepine site for short-term insomnia; still cause dependence.
An older sedative (e.g. phenobarbital) enhancing GABA with no overdose ceiling and potent enzyme induction — largely replaced by safer drugs.
Older 'typical' antipsychotics that strongly block D2 — effective but with more EPS and tardive dyskinesia (e.g. haloperidol).
Newer 'atypical' antipsychotics with combined D2/5-HT2A blockade — less EPS but more metabolic risk (e.g. olanzapine).
Drugs that raise acetylcholine by blocking its breakdown — first-line for Alzheimer dementia (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine).
A drug blocking the glutamate NMDA receptor; memantine (dementia) and ketamine/esketamine (depression) work this way.
A drug depleting presynaptic dopamine by blocking its vesicular transporter — used for tardive dyskinesia and chorea (tetrabenazine).
A dual orexin receptor antagonist blocking wake-promoting orexin to treat insomnia without GABAergic dependence (e.g. lemborexant).
An alpha-2 adrenergic agonist reducing sympathetic outflow — used in ADHD (guanfacine, clonidine) and agitation (dexmedetomidine).
Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor — bupropion; activating, no sexual side effects, but lowers the seizure threshold.
Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — a non-stimulant option for ADHD (atomoxetine) with no misuse potential.
Serotonin antagonist/reuptake inhibitor — trazodone; sedating via alpha-1 and H1 blockade, used low-dose for insomnia.
A steroid that modulates GABA-A receptors; brexanolone and zuranolone use this mechanism for postpartum depression.
